title: Multi-Qubit Systems

theory.introduction: When we combine multiple qubits, we enter the domain of multi-qubit systems. These systems unlock the most powerful quantum phenomena — entanglement, interference, and genuine quantum parallelism. A system of n qubits can represent 2^n states simultaneously, giving quantum computers their exponential power.
theory.start_from_zero: A single qubit has two basis states: |0⟩ and |1⟩. When we have two qubits, we combine them using a mathematical operation called the tensor product. This gives us four basis states: |00⟩, |01⟩, |10⟩, and |11⟩.
theory.why_it_matters: Multi-qubit systems are where quantum computing becomes truly powerful. Entanglement, which exists only in multi-qubit systems, allows correlations that are impossible classically. All practical quantum algorithms — Grover's, Shor's, QFT — operate on multi-qubit systems.
theory.core_idea: Multiple qubits are described by a joint state vector in a combined Hilbert space. The joint state is the tensor product of individual qubit states — unless the qubits are entangled, in which case the joint state cannot be factored.
key_points: n qubits span a 2^n dimensional state space
State of multiple qubits is described using tensor products
Entangled states cannot be written as a product of individual qubit states
The CNOT gate is the fundamental two-qubit entangling gate
Measurement of one qubit can instantly determine the state of another (entanglement)

tensor_product.description: The tensor product combines two quantum states into a joint state. For two qubits each in state |0⟩, the joint state is |0⟩ ⊗ |0⟩ = |00⟩.
tensor_product.general_rule: For qubit A in state α|0⟩ + β|1⟩ and qubit B in state γ|0⟩ + δ|1⟩, the joint state is αγ|00⟩ + αδ|01⟩ + βγ|10⟩ + βδ|11⟩

entanglement.description: When two qubits are entangled, their states cannot be described independently. Measuring one qubit instantly determines the outcome of measuring the other, regardless of distance.
entanglement.example: The Bell state (|00⟩ + |11⟩)/√2 cannot be written as a product of two single-qubit states.
entanglement.creation: Apply a Hadamard gate to qubit 0, then a CNOT with qubit 0 as control and qubit 1 as target.

cnot_gate.description: The Controlled-NOT (CNOT) gate is the most important two-qubit gate. It flips the target qubit if and only if the control qubit is |1⟩.
cnot_gate.use_case: Creating entanglement and acting as a quantum XOR gate

sections.title: State Space Grows Exponentially
content: 300 qubits → more states than atoms in the observable universe

sections.title: Bell States (Maximally Entangled Pairs)
content: These are the four maximally entangled two-qubit states

sections.title: Creating Entanglement Step by Step
content: Step 1: Start with both qubits in |0⟩ → |00⟩
Step 4: This is the Bell state Φ+ — perfectly entangled!

examples.title: Creating a Bell State
examples.description: Apply Hadamard to qubit 0, then CNOT to create the maximally entangled Bell state.
examples.insight: After this circuit, both qubits are perfectly correlated. Measuring either one instantly determines the state of the other.

examples.title: Two Independent Qubits in Superposition
examples.description: Apply Hadamard to both qubits independently.
examples.insight: All four states have equal probability. The qubits are not entangled — their states are independent.

comparisons.product_state: Can be written as product of individual qubit states
comparisons.entangled_state: Cannot be factored into individual qubit states

comparisons.product_state: Measuring one qubit gives no info about the other
comparisons.entangled_state: Measuring one qubit instantly determines the other

common_mistakes: Thinking entanglement allows faster-than-light communication (it doesn't)
Confusing tensor product with regular multiplication
Assuming all multi-qubit states are entangled
Forgetting that CNOT only flips the target when control is |1⟩
Thinking 2 qubits can only represent 2 values — they represent 4 amplitudes

try_this: Observe that |01⟩ and |10⟩ have zero probability — a signature of entanglement
Try applying H to both qubits without CNOT — notice all 4 states appear
Run multiple times — results are always correlated (both 0 or both 1)

quiz.question: How many basis states does a 3-qubit system have?
quiz.explanation: n qubits give 2^n basis states. For 3 qubits: 2^3 = 8.

quiz.question: What gate is used to create entanglement between two qubits?
quiz.explanation: The CNOT gate (combined with Hadamard) creates entanglement by making the target qubit dependent on the control qubit.

quiz.question: What is the Bell state (|00⟩ + |11⟩)/√2?
quiz.explanation: The Bell state cannot be written as a product of two individual qubit states — it is maximally entangled.

quiz.question: What does the CNOT gate do when the control qubit is |0⟩?
options: Does nothing to the target qubit
quiz.answer: Does nothing to the target qubit
quiz.explanation: CNOT only flips the target qubit when the control is |1⟩. When control is |0⟩, target is unchanged.

quiz.question: Which operation combines two qubit states into a joint state?
quiz.explanation: The tensor product (⊗) combines individual qubit states into a joint multi-qubit state.
